data.title: Charity leader’s faith in humanity renewed as 294 pets rescued in Hong Kong fire
data.description:
Almost 300 pets have been rescued from the deadly inferno that engulfed a Hong Kong housing estate last week, including fish, parrots and turtles, with a charity leader saying the rescue efforts have “strengthened her belief in humanity”.
The Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals said in a social media post that as of 8pm on Wednesday, 364 animals had been found since the fire, including 294 rescued alive and 70 discovered dead.
A total of 173 pets remained missing, bringing the estimated number of affected animals to over 537, according to the SPCA’s data, which combined information received from the Fire Services Department and various volunteers and groups.
The latest update included fish and pet crabs rescued over the past two days, as well as one deceased cat.
The fire at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, which started last Wednesday, raged for 43 hours across seven of its eight blocks.
Police said the Disaster Victim Identification Unit had since completed searches of all seven affected blocks and recovered the remains of three more victims, raising the total number of dead to 159.
Authorities have identified 140 of them, with 19 still pending confirmation.
Dozens of volunteers from animal protection groups were seen in the first few days caring for animals rescued from the fire with many bringing extra cages and carriers to provide temporary shelters.
A memorial zone dedicated to animals that perished in the blaze was set up at the Kwong Fuk sitting-out area, filled with messages expressing grief and prayers.
Sharon Pong Kwok Sau-wan, the founder of Hong Kong Parrot Rescue, said she arrived at Wang Fuk Court’s area last Wednesday, several hours after the fire broke out.
The initiative, which focuses on rescuing pet parrots and improving their lives by educating owners and carers, is part of her non-profit wildlife conservation organisation AquaMeridian.
She and other volunteers from the Animal Welfare Advocacy Alliance, which comprises many other local animal rescuers, remained at the fire scene for several days to manage both live and deceased pets.
“Nobody could foresee such a disaster. We were ill-prepared and could only do our best to make a positive difference,” said Kwok. “The disaster has strengthened my belief in humanity and made me even more proud to be a Hongkonger.”
The team spent the first night waiting near the fire site, positioned far enough away not to obstruct firefighters, watching survivors being wheeled out on stretchers while victims were carried away in black bags.

The first animal rescued was an Amazon parrot named Sprite, followed by several cats and a tortoise. Different animal charities were assigned to care for different species, Kwok said.
Sprite the parrot was still in an oxygen tank at Tai Wai Exotic Clinic, along with a Savanna monitor, a lizard native to Africa. Sprite was expected to be released soon, but the monitor had to be euthanised. He had been found at the site, already emaciated and dehydrated because of poor husbandry.
Kwok was also contacted by a young woman named Kimmy and her parents, residents of the estate who owned three parrots, for help in rescuing their birds.
The family had stepped out to investigate a commotion and were later denied re-entry.
Kwok recalled Kimmy’s face was constantly awash with tears, but there was nothing anyone could do except wait and pray.
The three parrots were eventually found safe, cared for overnight by the charity, and later reunited with their owners in smaller cages until their original ones could be replaced.
The team is also looking after five other birds, a chameleon, a ball python, a gecko, a leafy gecko and a large Sulcata tortoise until their owners can take them back.
Kwok said she barely returned home in the following days, only managing brief showers and naps. She expressed gratitude for the kindness of nearby residents who brought hot soup and tea, and for the empathy shared among strangers.
Police maintained order and restricted access to danger zones, while firefighters continued risking their lives – one fatally – to save others.
One firefighter, Kwok recalled, was drenched in sweat after carrying a single cage containing seven cats down from above the 20th floor.
“I’m sure that our society and all the departments involved feel the pain and loss from this tragedy deeply and will work towards much improved safety standards,” said Kwok.
“May the departed rest in peace and the survivors cherish each moment with their loved ones.”

data.title: South Korea parliament hosts ‘dark tour’ on martial law crisis anniversary
data.description: December 3 was the one-year anniversary of then-President Yoon Suk-yeol’s temporary declaration of martial law in South Korea.\nTo mark the occasion, more than 200 people were led on a tour of the National Assembly, which was seized by troops during the political crisis that shook the country.\n

data.title: Hong Kong lawyer who had sex with girl, 12, jailed for 3 years, 7 months
data.description:
A Hong Kong lawyer has been jailed for three years and seven months for having sex twice with a 12-year-old girl and taking more than 200 photographs and videos of the illegal acts.
The District Court on Thursday rejected the claim by Kelvin Luk Kin-ting, 40, that he was in a serious and genuinely loving relationship with the girl, finding instead that he had preyed on her immaturity and curiosity for sex to satisfy his own desires.
Deputy Judge Katherine Lo Kit-yee said she was dismayed that a well-educated lawyer such as Luk would solicit sex from a Form One pupil with limited life experience and exposure to society.
Lo said the case was aggravated by Luk’s insistence on having sex with the girl without a condom, using the excuse that he was allergic to the protective covering.
The court heard Luk, a former consultant at local law firm HY Leung & Co LLP, became infatuated with the girl – identified in court as X – after failed relationships with adult women.
When the pair first met on the dating app “Heymandi” in January last year, Luk claimed to be aged about 28 or 29, while the girl portrayed herself as a senior secondary school student preparing for university entrance exams.
They continued communicating and met for the first time on March 4, both realising they had not been truthful about their age.
That morning, the lawyer kissed the girl on a beach in Ma Wan before taking her to his high-rise flat at the nearby Park Island residential complex, where they had sex.
They met again at the same flat on March 28, with the girl agreeing to have sex with him after drinking a few cans of beer.
Luk upset the girl by removing his condom midway through intercourse but decided to continue with the act.
He took 204 pictures and nine videos of the girl while she was naked during the two encounters.
The lawyer’s crimes came to light in May 2024 after the girl’s father discovered that she had sent her naked photographs to the accused.
Luk pleaded guilty last month to two counts of unlawful sexual intercourse with a girl under 13, two of making child pornography and one of procuring a person under 16 for producing pornography.
A clinical psychologist opined that the lawyer became infatuated with the girl as he was able to connect with her emotionally.
The court heard the girl, now 14, did not exhibit any post-traumatic stress disorder symptoms but appeared depressed and displayed a tendency to avoid the topic of sex in daily conversations.
Defence counsel called for leniency and cited text messages that suggested the school pupil had tried to procure sex from the defendant.
Deputy Judge Lo, however, said Luk took a much more proactive role in the illicit relationship, repeatedly trying to engage the girl in discussions about sex and meet her in person more frequently.
Lo noted that none of the lawyer’s conversations with the girl concerned their personal lives and hobbies as those expected of couples in long and stable relationships.
“This court does not accept there was any intention by the defendant to have a genuinely loving relationship with X,” she said.
While a clinical psychologist assessed Luk as having a low risk of reoffending, Lo said the finding offered little mitigating value because the assessment was primarily based on what the defendant said about himself in a favourable light.
The court sentenced Luk to between 9½ months and 35 months for each offence, for a total term of 43 months.

data.title: Malaysian man stranded by floods in Thailand with ‘colleagues’ exposed holidaying with lover
data.description:
A Malaysian man who informed his wife that he was on a business trip with colleagues was discovered to be stranded with his mistress amid severe floods in Thailand.
Heavy flooding, triggered by torrential rains, has struck southern Thailand, claiming over 80 lives and affecting three million people across 12 provinces, including Hat Yai, the largest city in southern Thailand.
On November 24, a Malaysian woman, who had been sharing rescue updates in Hat Yai, posted that a distressed Malaysian wife sought her assistance to locate her husband, who she believed was trapped in the city with his colleagues.

She mentioned that their young children were anxiously awaiting their father’s return.
The woman, known by the handle @psmommyhannah, enlisted the help of her relatives living in the city to search for the man at the hotel where he was supposedly staying.
To their shock, they discovered that the man’s “colleagues” were, in fact, a woman who had been sharing the hotel room with him for the past four days.
The poster revealed that she had not yet broken the news to the wife, who is pregnant with their fourth child.
She stated that her intention in sharing the story was not to seek attention but to remind wives to “be cautious and not trust our husbands too much.”
According to her, the wife had no suspicions, as the husband frequently communicated with her.

The post garnered 5,100 likes and over 500 comments.
One woman urged that the wife deserves to know the truth: “I hope you tell her, no matter how painful it may be.”
Another suggested that the poster could inform a family member or friend of the wife, who could then speak to her directly.
Grateful for the advice, she stated she would attempt to find contact information for the wife’s family.
This was not the only case of a Malaysian husband whose deception was exposed due to the Hat Yai floods.
According to the Malaysian news outlet Gempak, a 45-year-old man misled his wife by claiming he was attending “an important meeting” in Kuala Lumpur, while in reality, he had travelled to Hat Yai with friends to “clear his mind.”

When he became stranded by the floods, he tried to further deceive his wife by asserting that Kuala Lumpur was also beset by chest-deep floods.
The man inadvertently revealed the truth himself when he accidentally shared his live location with his wife while updating her on his situation.
In contrast to the first case, the wife reportedly had already suspected his claims before receiving the message.
“I noticed he had packed Hawaiian shirts and shorts while telling me he had a meeting in Kuala Lumpur. Since when does Kuala Lumpur have a beach?” she remarked.

data.title: The Peninsula New York brings Christmas to glittering life on Fifth Avenue
data.description:
Few cities do Christmas like New York. If you’re a fan of the holiday season, you’d be hard pressed to find a better place to soak up the seasonal cheer than Midtown Manhattan and its main thoroughfare, Fifth Avenue. Come Thanksgiving weekend, the boulevard becomes Christmas Central. With the holiday windows of luxury department stores like Bergdorf Goodman and Saks Fifth Avenue, the Rockefeller Center Christmas tree and ice rink, and not to mention the Radio City Christmas Spectacular and shows like The Nutcracker – you could spend a whole week immersing yourself in all things Christmas.
Located on the corner of Fifth Avenue and 55th Street, a short walk from Rockefeller Center, Central Park and the Museum of Modern Art, The Peninsula New York makes for a wonderful base during the holidays - or any time you visit New York, for that matter.

In 1988, the Hong Kong-based hotel chain took over a landmark Beaux-Arts structure that was first built in 1905 and originally operated as the Gotham Hotel. Since then, The Peninsula New York has set the bar as one of the city’s top luxury properties, bringing Asian hospitality to one of the world’s most dynamic destinations.

In September 2024, the hotel emerged from an extensive renovation led by New York City-based Bill Rooney Studio. The unveiling revealed accommodations and public areas that were refreshed and modernised with contemporary furniture and hi-tech amenities while still maintaining design touches harking back to the building’s art deco heritage.

From the imposing staircase that greets guests upon arrival to the incredible views of the city’s skyline from Pen Top, its rooftop bar, the hotel is always buzzing with stylish locals and in-the-know travellers. But it also provides plenty of room for relaxation and quiet.
Its stunning pool area, with its soaring skylight ceiling, is a haven of calm while the 35,000 sq ft Peninsula Spa is one of New York’s top destinations for wellness and beauty. You can indulge in facials by cult skincare label Biologique Recherche or soothing body massages with oils from aromatherapy brand Subtle Energies.

Dining options include Clement, a modern eatery with a menu inspired by the Northeastern US, and the more casual Gotham Lounge, a lovely spot for a quick bite or afternoon tea.

New York has its fair share of five-star hotels, from grand dames such as The St Regis, which sits just across the street on Fifth Avenue, to the recently renovated Waldorf-Astoria, also located nearby. But if you’re looking for Asian-style attentive service combined with all the bells and whistles of Fifth Avenue grandeur, The Peninsula New York is one of those classics that will never get old and remains a wonderful place to return to again and again.
The author stayed as a guest of The Peninsula New York.

data.title: Beijing ‘absolutely does not accept’ Japanese leader’s seeming Taiwan climbdown
data.description:
Beijing has dismissed Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s latest remarks on Taiwan as “prevarication”, warning that it “absolutely does not accept” her continued refusal to retract comments made in early November.
On Wednesday, Takaichi appeared to attempt to dial down weeks of tensions when she told a parliamentary questioning session that Tokyo’s “fundamental position regarding Taiwan remains unchanged from that stated in the 1972 Japan-China Joint Communique, and there has been no change whatsoever to this position”.
However, she did not mention the contents of that document. She also did not, despite Beijing’s repeated insistence, withdraw her November 7 comment to parliament that an attack on Taiwan by the People’s Liberation Army could constitute a “survival-threatening situation” for Japan – one that could allow Tokyo to mobilise its military.
Those remarks made Takaichi the first Japanese leader since World War II to publicly link a Taiwan contingency with the possible deployment of Japan’s Self-Defence Forces, and soon sent relations with Beijing into a downward spiral.


The day after her purported clarification, Beijing insisted that Japan “correct its wrongdoing”, indicating that nothing short of a retraction would suffice.
“Prime Minister Takaichi is still prevaricating by claiming that the Japanese side’s position remains unchanged. China absolutely does not accept this,” foreign ministry spokesman Lin Jian said on Thursday when asked about her latest comment.
“China’s attitude is clear. We urged the Japanese side to earnestly reflect on and correct its wrongdoing and retract Prime Minister Takaichi’s erroneous remarks.”
Lin also urged Takaichi to “accurately and fully” spell out in her public addresses what was stated in the 1972 communique, which led to the normalisation of China-Japan ties.
“Why does the Japanese side just refuse to clearly articulate the commitments it has made and its legal obligations,” Lin added. “What is the logic and agenda behind this?”
The communique states that “the government of the People’s Republic of China reiterates that Taiwan is an inalienable part of the territory of the People’s Republic of China” and the Japanese government “fully understands and respects this stand … and it firmly maintains its stand under Article 8 of the Potsdam Proclamation” .
The clause, part of the defining terms of Japan’s World War II surrender, also known as the Potsdam Declaration, reads: “the terms of the Cairo Declaration shall be carried out and Japanese sovereignty shall be limited to the islands of Honshu, Hokkaido, Kyushu, Shikoku and such minor islands as we determine”.
The Cairo Declaration – signed in 1943 by China, the United States and the United Kingdom – stated that “all the territories Japan has stolen from the Chinese, such as Manchuria, Formosa, and the Pescadores, shall be restored to” China and that “Japan will also be expelled from all other territories which she has taken by violence and greed”.
Formosa was the Portuguese name for the island of Taiwan and was widely used in the West until the mid-20th century.

data.title: New Delhi records more than 200,000 respiratory cases due to toxic air
data.description: New Delhi, with its sprawling metropolitan region of 30 million residents, is regularly ranked among the world’s most polluted capitals. The city recorded more than 200,000 cases of acute respiratory illness at six state-run hospitals between 2022 and 2024, government numbers showed, highlighting the adverse effects of toxic air on health.\n

data.title: Hong Kong abruptly cancels 3 Japanese film screenings amid diplomatic row
data.description:
Amid souring Sino-Japanese relations, Hong Kong’s Leisure and Cultural Services Department (LCSD) has abruptly cancelled three screenings of Japanese films for its upcoming film programme.
The decision was announced on December 3, three days before “Food for Thought – a Cinematic Feast” was slated to begin.
Kamome Diner (2006), previously scheduled for December 6 and January 3, follows a woman working in a quiet cafe who eventually forms an unexpected family with two other solitary women.
Tampopo (1985), a cult comedy by director Juzo Itami that was initially scheduled for December 28 and January 11, depicts a truck driver who trains a widowed noodle shop owner in the rigorous art of making the perfect bowl of ramen.
Also cancelled is Naomi Kawase’s An (2015), known internationally as Sweet Bean, which was originally planned as a free screening on January 18.
Screenings of other “Food for Thought” films from other corners of the world, including India, France, the UK, the US and mainland China, are scheduled to proceed as planned.

The programme, run by the LCSD’s Film Programmes Office, was curated by Ernest Chan Chi-wa, an executive committee member and former president of the Hong Kong Film Critics Society.
Spanning various genres, the films – planned to include showings at the Hong Kong Film Archive (HKFA) as well as K11 Art House – had been curated by Chan to guide the audience through touching stories where food is not only a culinary art but also a journey into people’s hearts and inner worlds.
In a short statement on Chan’s Facebook page, he expressed deep sadness for the cancellations.
Responding to a query from the Post, Chan said that he only found out on December 3 that the screenings had been cancelled, and that he did not know specific details. The official web pages for both the LCSD and HKFA do not mention the exact reason behind the cancellations. As of publication, the LCSD has yet to comment.
Fiona Law Yuk-wa, who specialises in Hong Kong and Asian cinema, was originally scheduled to deliver a post-screening talk for Kamome Diner on December 6.
“I was only informed yesterday that the screening was cancelled … no reason was given to me by the [Film Programmes Office],” Law told the Post.

Disheartened by the cancellation, she feels that “the programme wonderfully includes different food-related films from all over the world, mapping a delicious foodscape that may help restore energy to people in Hong Kong … and showing the city as a world-class cultural hub”.
“Missing these three films is like having an incomplete meal with some nutrients lacking,” she adds.
The LCSD, a key cultural institution in Hong Kong, often organises diverse thematic film series.
Its Film Programmes Office has a long history of collaborating with foreign consulates general and cultural organisations to present international cinema, including the city’s French Film Festival, the German Film Forum and past sponsored programmes with the consulate general of Japan.
Sino-Japanese tensions have escalated to their lowest point in years, primarily driven by Japan’s increasingly vocal stance on Taiwan.
The recent diplomatic crisis was ignited by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s November 7 remarks suggesting that a Chinese attack on Taiwan would be deemed a “survival-threatening situation” to Japan, potentially warranting military response under collective self-defence.
In retaliation, China has employed various forms of economic and cultural coercion, including issuing travel advisories against Japan, imposing a ban on Japanese seafood imports, and freezing licensing reviews for Japanese films and concerts.

The chill was felt on November 28 in Shanghai during a performance by Japanese singer Maki Otsuki at the Bandai Namco Festival. Midway through her rendition of “Memories”, the ending theme for One Piece, the venue abruptly cut the lights and sound, forcing staff to rush the stage and escort her off.
Also in Shanghai, a concert by Japanese singer Ayumi Hamasaki was cancelled less than 24 hours before its scheduled start on November 29.
Kokia saw her Beijing concert cancelled, though her shows in Guangzhou, Shenzhen and Chengdu went ahead as scheduled.
Dozens of concerts by other Japanese artists have also been cancelled across China.
There does not appear to be a blanket ban on Japanese programmes in Hong Kong, where pianist Mao Fujita and violinist Midori performed sold-out concerts in the past week.
Spokespeople for the West Kowloon Cultural District and the Hong Kong Arts Festival say that they have not received any instruction to cancel shows with Japanese performers.

data.title: How ‘housewives heartthrob’ Moses Chan became one of Hong Kong’s top TV stars
data.description:
Moses Chan Ho is one of the most recognisable stars on television in Hong Kong, having appeared in TV hits such as War and Beauty (2004) and Heart of Greed (2007). These days, he is also the face of countless commercial services and products, ranging from private banking to health supplements.
The 54-year-old’s career is built on a foundation of professionalism and quiet dedication, reflecting a steady climb rather than a meteoric rise. It has also led the celebrated actor into the coffee world, where he has become an entrepreneur.
Chan’s early life hinted at a different future. Born in 1971 into a well-to-do family, his path seemed set when he moved to Brisbane, Australia, and enrolled in university to study accounting.
His foray into the world of entertainment began not in front of a camera, but on the runway, when he was scouted to be a model for Esprit after his graduation. This led him back to Hong Kong, where he starred in his first film, cult classic Twenty Something (1994). However, his cinematic career sputtered, and a series of minor, forgettable roles followed.

The turning point came in 2000 with his move to the Hong Kong broadcasting company TVB, where he quickly became a familiar face, landing big roles in a string of critically and commercially successful series.
“I’d only played gangsters before my first role as a doctor on [2000 series] Healing Hands II. I remember I kept walking into scenes with my hands in my pockets to act cool,” Chan tells the Post.
From the historical drama Where the Legend Begins (2002) to the palace intrigue War and Beauty (2004) to the family feud drama The Gem of Life (2008), Chan showed a remarkable range.
It was his role as the flawed but charismatic Cao Pi in Where the Legend Begins that first made audiences and critics sit up and take notice, earning him his first acting award in 2002 and the moniker of “see lai killer” – a heartthrob for housewives.
The role, as the eldest son of the legendary warlord Cao Cao during the Three Kingdoms period (220-280), is also Chan’s most memorable.
“I was a bunch of nerves and had like 20 to 30 retakes,” he recalls. “I really have to thank [fellow actors] Ada [Choi Siu-fun] and Steven [Ma Chun-wai] for being so patient. I look back on this show with the fondest memories for not only the actors but also the production and the script.”
The year 2007 marked a watershed moment for Chan when he was named best actor at the TVB Anniversary Awards for his portrayal of Teddy in the family blockbuster Heart of Greed. That same night, he also won the “My Favourite Male Character” award, achieving a rare “Double Crown” victory. Subsequent acting wins around the region led to the expansion of the title to “Quadruple Crown” holder.
Asked whether he prefers historical or modern dramas, he says he would choose historical ones.
“I’m old-fashioned that way. I think anyone who enjoys acting would feel there are similarities between the historical dramas and Shakespeare’s works. The contrast is not in the plot but rather in the nuances, the way the characters are developed … there is just more space to play with the characters.
“In contrast, modern dramas require a more natural and sincere approach. You can be a lot more dramatic in historical dramas.”
Chan’s long filming hours have not only produced an accomplished actor, but also a coffee aficionado. In an effort to better understand the craft, Chan became a certified barista and coffee taster, dedicating himself to learning everything from roasting to brewing.
In 2018, he co-founded Hong Kong coffee brand Blooms Coffee, aiming to serve high-quality brew made with ethically sourced beans.
He credits his passion for coffee to his upbringing in Australia.
“I loved the coffee culture when I lived in Australia,” he says. “I inherited the slower pace and laid-back attitude from growing up there. From drinking coffee to making my own coffee to paying attention to the brewing and bean roasting process all accumulated to my venture into the coffee industry.”

Today, Chan is known as a consummate professional and a devoted family man, something that endears him to the public. His marriage to actress Aimee Chan Yan-mei is a central part of his life, as are their three children.
“A home is happier with children,” Chan explains. “I always wanted to be a father. I thought I would be a father in my thirties, but it didn’t happen until 10 years later.”
Asked if he had any regrets about being an older father, he says it all worked out for him.
“A man in his 20s doesn’t know what he’s doing,” he says. “By their mid-thirties, things usually start to settle down, and they find their life course. If they’re lucky, they will find someone to marry and have kids, and by their forties, they will concentrate on their family and career, and hopefully they can enjoy their golden years in their fifties.”
Ultimately, it is family, not show business, that is firmly anchored at the centre of Chan’s life.
“I found that I got married when I found the direction in my life; it made me a better provider, and I can take care of my family better,” he says.
“If I hadn’t figured out who I was, it would have been very hard for me to take up the responsibility. Of course, as an older father, I find it harder to keep up with their inexhaustible energy, but anything I do with my kids, whether it’s swimming or cycling, it’s just pure happiness.”

data.title: South Korea, Thailand emerge as top choice for Chinese tourists amid Japan tensions
data.description:
With rising tensions between Beijing and Tokyo disrupting travel plans, Chinese tourists are actively seeking alternative destinations for their winter getaways. While South Korea hopes to capitalise on this diverted tourist flow, it faces fierce competition from popular hotspots like Thailand, Russia and other destinations, according to travel industry officials.
The diplomatic spat began when Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi suggested potential military intervention in the Taiwan Strait, prompting Beijing to issue a travel warning on November 14.
Chinese airlines were instructed to offer refunds on Japan-bound flights through the end of the year, leaving hundreds of thousands of Chinese travellers scrambling to adjust their plans.
The timing aligns with South Korean airlines’ efforts to expand routes to China, which are more profitable than routes to Japan. Korean Air Lines increased its Incheon-Fuzhou route from three weekly flights to four last month, and Asiana Airlines is set to operate 165 weekly flights to China by March, reflecting a 20 per cent increase in capacity.
Chinese cruise operators are also scrambling to bypass Japanese ports, opting instead to extend their stays at destinations such as Jeju Island.

Data from Chinese travel platform Qunar revealed that for the weekend of November 15, South Korea emerged as the most popular overseas destination for Chinese tourists, overtaking Japan, which previously held the top spot.
It also ranked first in flight ticket payments and search volume, followed by Thailand, Hong Kong, Malaysia, Singapore, Vietnam and Indonesia.
According to data from the Korea Tourism Organisation, 4.7 million Chinese tourists visited South Korea between January and October this year, exceeding the total number of visitors for last year.
But even as Korea hopes to benefit from the lingering tensions between China and Japan, several travel agencies said that Chinese tourists have an abundance of alternative travel destinations, even as interest in visiting South Korea continues to grow.
Some Chinese travellers are increasingly opting for destinations that are visa-free, such as Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, Georgia and Egypt, according to Li Xiaoya, a Beijing-based travel agent.
Most group tours’ flights to Japan have been cancelled, and we’re instead promoting packages for other countries and regions
Li Xiaoya, Beijing-based travel agent
“Most group tours’ flight tickets to Japan have been cancelled, and we’re now instead promoting packages for other countries and regions, such as Thailand, Semporna [in Malaysia], South Korea and others,” she said.
According to data released by Tongcheng Travel on Monday, hotel bookings by Chinese tourists for trips to South Korea in the last two weeks of November surged by over 240 per cent year-on-year.
Hotel bookings for Southeast Asian destinations such as Vietnam and Indonesia also saw year-on-year growth exceeding 100 per cent, while bookings for Germany and Spain showed an impressive year-on-year increase of more than 300 per cent.
Russia is also favoured by Chinese, thanks to the implementation of visa-free policies for up to 30 days starting this month. Chinese media reported that searches and bookings for trips to Russia surged following the announcement of the visa waiver.
Data released by Qunar last week revealed that Thailand has become the top destination for Chinese outbound tourists during the January 15 to February 10, 2026, winter holiday period. Meanwhile, Russia has experienced a significant surge in popularity, with flight bookings increasing 1.5 times year-on-year.
A staff member at a travel agency catering to Chinese tourists in South Korea noted a slight uptick in winter bookings following the China-Japan tensions. However, the increase has been modest, likely because January and February are not peak travel months for South Korea, he said.
“There are numerous options for Chinese tourists looking to experience winter landscapes, including domestic destinations like Harbin or international locations such as Russia and various European countries. South Korea is facing stiff competition in this area,” the staff member said.

A sales representative from a Shanghai-based Chinese airline revealed that the carrier initially planned to reduce flight capacity to Thailand in November and December. However, due to the ongoing tensions with Japan, they decided to increase capacity instead.
“Destinations like Sanya [in Hainan province], and Thailand usually experience some year-on-year growth among Chinese tourists during the winter season,” the representative added.
Meanwhile, South Korea remains an appealing destination for travellers from cities like Shanghai.
Willow Zhao, a 27-year-old doctoral student in Shanghai who recently cancelled her trip to Japan, said she is actively considering South Korea due to its convenient location and the allure of duty-free shopping.
However, South Korea’s visa policy may deter individual tourists adjusting to sudden travel plan changes. Currently, only group tours qualify for visa-free access. Xie Lumei, an accountant from Fujian province, said that she initially wanted to visit Seoul but decided against it, unwilling to deal with the hassle of a visa application.
This story was first published by The Korea Times
